title: After Action Review — Coaching Substitution System (Backup Player Rotation)
scope: Implement a coaching substitution system so backup players actually see the field during games. Prior to this change, substitutions only occurred when a player was injured mid-game.

rationale.item: Backup zerobacks across the league averaged 0 game touches per season. Depth players existed only as insurance against injuries that rarely happened.
A starter could fumble 5 times and keep playing. A halfback with 25 carries and depleted energy would never get a breather. In a 50-point blowout, starters played every snap of Q4.

delegation_events.quote: Real coaches don't yank a halfback off the field between plays. Substitutions happen at natural stoppages — between drives, at halftime.
A starter with 12 carries in Q3 has a 15% rest chance, not a guaranteed benching. Different coaching philosophies: some coaches ride their workhorse, others rotate aggressively.

delegation_events.decision: Use workload (carries/touches) not energy system as fatigue proxy
delegation_events.quote: The per-player energy system keeps starters near 100 energy for most of the game. Workload (carries, touches) is a much better proxy for 'this player should rest' than the energy value itself.

delegation_events.decision: _unavailable_in_game() wraps both injured and benched sets as single point of filtering
delegation_events.quote: Single point of filtering. Every method that selects players calls one function and gets back everyone who shouldn't be on the field, regardless of reason.

actions.description: Three substitution triggers (performance/workload/blowout), benched state with expiration rules, evaluate_coaching_substitutions() called between drives
New _home_benched/_away_benched dicts; _unavailable_in_game() wrapping both injured and benched sets; 12+ player selection methods updated
Substitution log output in game summary under coaching_substitutions key
_clear_halftime_benches() and bench expiration processing at drive start

validation_claims.claim: 20-game batch test (seed=99): fatigue/workload rests 20 (1.0/game), performance benchings 2 (0.1/game), blowout rests 50 (2.5/game), total 72 (3.6/game).
validation_claims.check_method: Run 20-game batch sim with seed=99 and count substitution types

validation_claims.claim: Emergent: comeback catalyst — Saint Mary's led by 25+ in Q3 and pulled starters; Yale closed to 44.5-43.5. Multi-cycle rotation: Nneka Obi rested 3 separate times in one game.
validation_claims.check_method: Review game logs for blowout rest / comeback sequences

residual_risks: Blowout threshold (25 points) may be too conservative — a 20-point lead may be better threshold in a 9-point TD sport
Performance bench at halftime reset may cause yo-yo benching for same player fumbling twice
Backup quality gap may allow blowout losing teams to score freely against backups — may need explicit modifier
Defensive substitution frequency may need tuning (tackles≥10 in Q3, 25% chance × 50% confirmation)
section_headers_original: After Action Review — Coaching Substitution System (Backup Player Rotation)
Benching vs. Injury — Parallel But Reversible
unmapped_content: Branch: claude/add-backup-substitutions-tOe46. Commit: 5774c0a. 1 file changed, +255/-21 lines. Three trigger table: performance (2+ fumbles OR 3+ turnovers OR <1.5 YPC on 8+ carries at 40%), workload (12+ carries in Q3 15-40% scaling OR 18+ touches in Q4 30%), blowout (leading 25+ in Q3/Q4). Substitution log schema: {team, quarter, time_remaining, benched_player, benched_position, reason, duration, substitute}. Lessons: energy system too generous for substitution triggers; multiple triggers needed (injury-only subs meant backups never played in 95%+ games); benching is a coaching decision, not a player state.
parser_notes: Date confirmed from document header: 2026-03-20. 'What to Watch' section mapped to residual_risks.
